Loaded: V sags below E in proportion to the current — the headlights dim when the starter motor's huge I makes Ir bite. Short circuit (V = 0): the current maxes out at I = E/r, limited by nothing but the source's own innards — which is why shorting a car battery (tiny r, huge E/r) is a welding accident. Circuit analysis simply treats r as one more series resistor: I = E/(R + r), and every Kirchhoff loop through a real battery writes +E − Ir. Measurement inverts the story: plot terminal voltage against current drawn and V = E − Ir is a straight line — intercept E, slope −r — the standard experiment. Power bookkeeping completes it: the source converts EI per second, of which I²R lands in the load and I²r heats the battery (that is why hard-working batteries get warm). Maximizing LOAD power gives the maximum power transfer theorem — P_load peaks when R = r — but at only 50% efficiency, which is why power grids (efficiency first) run R ≫ r while signal electronics (transfer first) match impedances.
key_ideas: EMF = energy per unit charge supplied by the source (volts, not a force) — the chemistry's full price paid to each coulomb.
Internal resistance r: the source resists its own current; V_terminal = E − Ir — the terminals deliver the EMF minus the inside toll.
Three regimes: open circuit V = E (measure EMF at zero draw); loaded V < E (sag ∝ I); short circuit I_max = E/r (the source's own limit).
Circuit rule: treat r as a series resistor — I = E/(R + r); in Kirchhoff loops a real battery contributes +E and −Ir.
The V–I line: terminal voltage versus current is straight with intercept E and slope −r — the standard determination experiment.
Power split: EI total = I²R (load) + I²r (self-heating); load power peaks at R = r (maximum power transfer) but at 50% efficiency — grids choose efficiency (R ≫ r), signal circuits choose transfer.

common_misconceptions.misconception: EMF is a force that pushes charges around the circuit.
common_misconceptions.correction: Despite the name, EMF is ENERGY PER CHARGE (volts, i.e., joules per coulomb) — the work the source does lifting each coulomb through itself. Nothing with units of newtons is involved; 'electromotive force' is a 200-year-old naming accident the equations have outgrown.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A battery's terminal voltage always equals its EMF — that's what the label means.
common_misconceptions.correction: Only at ZERO current. Under load, V = E − Ir: the terminals deliver less than the label by the internal drop. A '12 V' car battery cranking 200 A through r = 0.01 Ω shows only 10 V at its posts — same battery, different current, different terminal voltage.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A battery is a constant-current source — it always pushes the same current.
common_misconceptions.correction: A battery approximates a constant-VOLTAGE source (EMF fixed by chemistry); the CURRENT is set by the whole circuit: I = E/(R + r). Halve the load resistance and the current nearly doubles. Nothing in the battery meters out fixed amperes.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Maximum power transfer (R = r) is the goal of every circuit design.
common_misconceptions.correction: At R = r, half the power cooks the source — 50% efficiency, intolerable for energy delivery. Power systems run R ≫ r for efficiency near 100%; matching R = r is chosen only where extracting the most SIGNAL power matters more than waste (antennas, audio lines). Know which game you're playing.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A battery cutaway schematic: an ideal EMF cell in series with a small resistor r inside one casing, terminals outside — the model that makes every equation obvious.
The three-regime animation: open circuit (V = E), increasing load (V sagging as Ir grows), short circuit (V = 0, I = E/r) — a terminal voltmeter and ammeter tracking throughout.
The V–I experiment plotted live: data points falling on a straight line, intercept flagged E, slope flagged −r.
An energy-ledger bar chart: EI total splitting into I²R (load, useful) and I²r (internal heat) as R is swept — with the R = r crossover and the efficiency curve overlaid.
The car-cranking storyboard: headlights at full V, starter engaged (200 A), Ir bite, lights dimming — the everyday face of internal resistance.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A cell of EMF 12.0 V and internal resistance 0.50 Ω is connected to a 5.5 Ω load. (a) Find the current and the terminal voltage. (b) Find the power delivered to the load and the power wasted inside the cell. (c) What current would flow if the terminals were accidentally shorted? (d) In a V–I test this cell gave V = 11.0 V at I = 2.0 A and V = 10.0 V at I = 4.0 A — verify E and r from the data.
steps: (b) Load: P_R = I²R = 4.0 × 5.5 = 22 W. Internal: P_r = I²r = 4.0 × 0.50 = 2.0 W. Total EI = 12.0 × 2.0 = 24 W ✓ — ledger closed; efficiency = 22/24 ≈ 92%.
(c) Short circuit: I_max = E/r = 12.0/0.50 = 24 A — five times the working current, limited only by the cell's interior; sustained, it cooks the cell (P_r = I²r = 288 W all inside).
(d) Two-point fit of V = E − Ir: slope = (10.0 − 11.0)/(4.0 − 2.0) = −0.50 → r = 0.50 Ω; intercept: E = V + Ir = 11.0 + 2.0 × 0.50 = 12.0 V ✓ — the graph method recovers both parameters.
Regime replay: open-circuit reading would be 12.0 V; the 11.0 V at 2.0 A is the loaded sag; 24 A is the short — one equation, three behaviors.

Design note: this cell wastes 8% at 2 A. Maximum LOAD power would need R = r = 0.5 Ω (I = 12 A, P_R = 72 W) — but then 72 W ALSO heats the cell: 50% efficiency, fine for a signal, ruinous for a power supply.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes I = 2.0 A and V = 11.0 V by two agreeing routes, closes the 24 W power ledger, finds the 24 A short-circuit limit, extracts E = 12.0 V and r = 0.50 Ω from V–I data, and distinguishes efficiency from maximum-transfer design goals.

item_types: I = E/(R + r) and V = E − Ir computations across the three regimes
V–I graph interpretation (intercept/slope extraction of E and r)
power-ledger problems (load versus internal, efficiency)
design questions (battery choice by r, series cells, maximum transfer versus efficiency)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with single-loop I and V computations, add the open/loaded/short regime contrasts, then graph-based determinations and power ledgers, ending with design trade-offs (R = r transfer versus R ≫ r efficiency) and multi-cell arrangements.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must compute with internal resistance across regimes, extract parameters from data, and apply the power-split analysis to design choices, beyond defining EMF.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student solves loaded-circuit problems with agreeing V routes, reads E and r off V–I lines, closes power ledgers, and correctly assigns maximum-transfer versus efficiency criteria to contexts, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Car batteries are engineered for milliohm internal resistance so 200 A cranking only mildly sags the terminals — and the headlight dim during starting is V = E − Ir performed nightly.
Phone battery health meters estimate r: as cells age, internal resistance climbs, voltage sags under load, and the phone dies at '30%' — the V–I line steepening with age.
Battery-powered tools pick chemistry by r: high-drain devices need low-r cells (lithium) where high-r ones (alkaline) would waste their EMF internally.
Impedance matching in audio and radio (R = r logic) maximizes signal power into speakers and antennas — the transfer theorem earning its keep where efficiency is secondary.
Uninterruptible power supplies and grid batteries are specified by both E and r — the deliverable power at acceptable sag is E²-and-r arithmetic before anything else.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to explain why capacitors charge and discharge exponentially, define and compute the time constant τ = RC, apply q(t), V(t), and I(t) for both charging and discharging, use the 63%/37% landmarks and half-life t½ = τ ln 2, and reason about capacitor behavior at t = 0 (acts like a wire) and t → ∞ (acts like a break).
assets.concept_summary: Put a resistor in series with a capacitor and the circuit acquires a MEMORY and a CLOCK. Close the switch on a battery E charging capacitor C through resistor R: Kirchhoff's loop law says E = IR + q/C at every instant — but as charge q accumulates, the capacitor's voltage rises and opposes the battery, shrinking the drive and hence the current that delivers further charge. Self-limiting growth is the signature of the EXPONENTIAL: q(t) = CE(1 − e^(−t/RC)), V_C(t) = E(1 − e^(−t/τ)), while the current starts at its maximum and dies away: I(t) = (E/R)e^(−t/τ). The governing scale is the TIME CONSTANT τ = RC (ohms × farads = seconds — a dimensional check worth performing once and believing forever): after one τ, charging reaches 63% of the way (1 − 1/e); after 2τ, 86%; 3τ, 95%; 5τ, over 99% — the engineer's 'fully charged'. Discharging is the mirror: q(t) = q₀e^(−t/τ), everything decaying by the factor 1/e ≈ 0.37 each τ, with the useful half-life t½ = τ ln 2 ≈ 0.693 RC (every half-life halves the charge — radioactive decay's exact mathematics, met here first in a circuit). Two limiting behaviors organize all qualitative reasoning: at t = 0 an uncharged capacitor is a WIRE (no voltage yet across it, current unimpeded, maximum I = E/R); as t → ∞ it is a BREAK (fully charged, opposing voltage equal to the source, zero current). Interrogate any RC circuit at these two instants and the exponential merely interpolates. The energy ledger from capacitance carries over with a twist: charging through ANY resistance banks ½CE² in the capacitor and burns an equal ½CE² in the resistor — the 50% tax is independent of R (R sets HOW LONG, not HOW MUCH). RC timing is everywhere: camera flashes recharging, intermittent wipers, the 555 timer, touchscreens sensing your finger's added C, and every heartbeat of a pacemaker.
key_ideas: Self-limiting growth: charging obeys E = IR + q/C; accumulating charge opposes the source, so growth slows itself — the defining mechanism of the exponential.
Time constant τ = RC (seconds): the circuit's natural clock — 63% charged after 1τ, 95% after 3τ, 'full' (>99%) after 5τ.
Charging: q = CE(1 − e^(−t/τ)), V_C likewise; current I = (E/R)e^(−t/τ) starts at max and dies — charge grows, current decays, simultaneously.
Discharging: pure decay q = q₀e^(−t/τ) — factor 0.37 per τ; half-life t½ = τ ln 2 ≈ 0.693RC halves everything each interval.
Limit reasoning: at t = 0 an empty capacitor acts as a WIRE (I = E/R); at t → ∞ as a BREAK (I = 0) — solve circuits at both ends, let the exponential bridge.
Energy: charging stores ½CE² and dissipates ½CE² in R regardless of R's value — resistance sets the timescale, never the split.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The capacitor charges at a steady rate until full, like filling a bucket from a steady tap.
common_misconceptions.correction: The tap throttles itself: as the capacitor's voltage rises it opposes the battery, the net drive (E − V_C) shrinks, and the charging current falls continuously — fast at first, ever slower. The approach is exponential, technically never complete; 5τ is the practical 'full'.

common_misconceptions.misconception: After one time constant the capacitor is fully charged.
common_misconceptions.correction: After 1τ it is 63% charged (1 − 1/e), not 100%. The τ landmarks are 63/86/95/99% at 1/2/3/5τ. One τ is the circuit's UNIT of time, not its finish line.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Current flows through the capacitor while it charges.
common_misconceptions.correction: No charge crosses the dielectric gap — ever. Current flows in the CIRCUIT: positive charge accumulates on one plate while an equal charge leaves the other, so the ammeter sees current 'through' the branch though nothing traverses the insulator. When accumulation stops, so does the current.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A bigger resistor means the capacitor stores less energy (since R eats more).
common_misconceptions.correction: R changes only the TIMESCALE. The final charge is CE and stored energy ½CE² whatever R is — and the resistor's total toll is ALSO fixed at ½CE² regardless of its value. Big R = same destination, slower trip, same total heat spread over more time.

visual_teaching_suggestions: The dual-curve plot: V_C(t) rising (1 − e^(−t/τ)) and I(t) falling (e^(−t/τ)) on shared axes, with the 1τ/63%, 3τ/95%, 5τ/99% landmarks flagged.
A self-throttling animation: battery pushing charge onto plates, the capacitor's back-voltage growing, the net drive arrow (E − V_C) visibly shrinking, current arrows thinning.
The two-limits diagram: the same circuit drawn at t = 0 (capacitor replaced by a wire) and t = ∞ (replaced by a break), each solved trivially.
A discharge half-life staircase: q dropping 100 → 50 → 25 → 12.5% at equal t½ intervals — the halving rhythm that radioactivity will repeat.
An RC timing gallery: intermittent wiper interval knob (varying R), camera-flash ready light, 555 timer — τ = RC as a designable product.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A 500 μF capacitor is charged through a 2.0 kΩ resistor from a 9.0 V battery, starting uncharged. (a) Find the time constant and the initial current. (b) Find the capacitor voltage at t = 1.0 s and t = 2.0 s. (c) How long until the capacitor is 95% charged, and what is the final stored charge and energy? (d) The battery is then removed and the capacitor discharges through the same resistor — find the half-life of the decay.
steps: (a) τ = RC = 2.0 × 10³ × 500 × 10⁻⁶ = 1.0 s (Ω·F = s ✓). Initial current (empty capacitor = wire): I₀ = E/R = 9.0/2000 = 4.5 mA.

(b) V_C(t) = 9.0(1 − e^(−t/1.0)). At t = 1.0 s (= 1τ): V = 9.0(1 − e⁻¹) = 9.0 × 0.632 = 5.7 V. At t = 2.0 s: V = 9.0(1 − e⁻²) = 9.0 × 0.865 = 7.8 V — the second second added only 2.1 V where the first added 5.7 V: self-throttling in numbers.
(c) 95% ↔ 3τ = 3.0 s. Final charge Q = CE = 500 × 10⁻⁶ × 9.0 = 4.5 × 10⁻³ C = 4.5 mC; stored energy U = ½CE² = ½ × 500 × 10⁻⁶ × 81 = 2.0 × 10⁻² J ≈ 20 mJ.
(Ledger note: the resistor dissipated an equal ~20 mJ during charging — the R-independent 50% tax; the 2.0 kΩ only made it take seconds rather than microseconds.)
(d) Discharge: q = q₀e^(−t/τ) with the same τ = 1.0 s. Half-life: t½ = τ ln 2 = 0.693 s — every 0.69 s the charge (and voltage, and current) halves: 9.0 → 4.5 V at 0.69 s, → 2.25 V at 1.39 s.
Limits audit: t = 0 gave I = E/R (wire); t → ∞ gives I = 0 and V_C = 9.0 V (break) — the exponential answers agree with both endpoints, as they must.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes τ = 1.0 s and I₀ = 4.5 mA, evaluates the charging curve at 1τ and 2τ (5.7 V, 7.8 V), converts 95% to 3τ = 3.0 s with Q = 4.5 mC and U = 20 mJ, and finds the discharge half-life 0.69 s, using the wire/break limits as endpoint checks.

item_types: τ = RC computations with unit verification and landmark-percentage questions
charging/discharging curve evaluations (find V, q, or I at given t; find t for given fraction)
limit-reasoning problems (circuit behavior at t = 0 and t → ∞, including multi-resistor networks)
half-life and energy-ledger items (t½ = τ ln 2, the ½CE²/½CE² split)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with τ computation and the 63/95/99 landmarks, add curve evaluations both directions (t → V and V → t via logarithms), then the two-limit analysis of richer circuits, ending with half-life chains and the R-independent energy-split argument.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must apply the exponential laws and time-constant landmarks to charging and discharging scenarios, and use the t = 0/t → ∞ limits to analyze circuits, beyond stating τ = RC.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student computes τ and evaluates both curves at arbitrary times (including inverse log solves), applies wire/break limit reasoning correctly, and reproduces the half-life and energy-split results, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Camera flashes are RC in both directions: slow charge through a big R (the whine and the ready light near 5τ), fast dump through the tube.
Intermittent windscreen wipers time their pause with an adjustable R in an RC network — the interval knob is literally a τ dial.
The 555 timer chip — billions made — clocks everything from LED blinkers to PWM motors by charging capacitors between fixed voltage thresholds.
Touchscreens sense your fingertip as added capacitance: the changed RC response time at each grid point maps the touch.
Cardiac pacemakers meter their life-keeping pulses with RC timing, and defibrillators are the discharge curve delivered clinically.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to describe the magnetic field as the agent of magnetic force with symbol B and unit tesla, draw and interpret field-line patterns (bar magnet, straight wire, loop), apply the right-hand rule for the field around a current, state that field lines form closed loops with no magnetic monopoles, and connect currents — not stationary charges — to magnetism.
assets.concept_summary: Magnetism gets its own field: B, the magnetic field, measured in tesla (T) — the agent that transmits magnetic influence through space exactly as E transmits electric influence. But B differs from E in its very source: while charges at REST make electric fields, magnetic fields are made by charges in MOTION — currents. That is the domain's hinge discovery (Oersted, 1820: a compass needle deflects beside a current-carrying wire — electricity and magnetism, separate sciences for centuries, unified in one lecture-hall accident). The field-line language carries over from electrostatics with the same grammar — direction along the tangent, strength in the crowding, lines never crossing — but with one profound structural change: magnetic field lines have NO ENDS. Electric lines run from + to −; magnetic lines form CLOSED LOOPS, because there are no magnetic charges — no monopoles. Cut a bar magnet in half and you get two smaller magnets, never a separated north and south; every north comes welded to a south, and 'poles' are just the places where loops crowd into or out of the material. The three canonical patterns are the topic's vocabulary: a BAR MAGNET's loops emerge from N, arc around, re-enter at S, and continue THROUGH the body back to N (the outside pattern matches a current loop's — a hint of magnetism's true source); a STRAIGHT WIRE wraps circles around itself, direction by the RIGHT-HAND RULE (thumb along the current, fingers curl along B), strength thinning with distance — quantitatively B = μ₀I/2πr, with μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A the permeability of free space (formula quoted here; its derivation is Biot–Savart's job later); a LOOP of current concentrates field through its face like a little bar magnet. Scale calibrates intuition: Earth's field ~5 × 10⁻⁵ T (30 A of current 5 cm away rivals it — why wiring confuses compasses), fridge magnets ~10⁻² T, MRI machines 1.5–3 T, and the tesla is huge. What B DOES — the force on moving charges, F = qv × B — is the next concept's whole story; here the mission is the field's existence, sources, and geometry.
key_ideas: B is the magnetic field: the space-filling agent of magnetic influence, unit the tesla (T = N/(A·m)) — a big unit; Earth's field is ~5 × 10⁻⁵ T.
Source hinge: MOVING charges (currents) create magnetic fields — stationary charges make only E. Oersted's deflected compass unified electricity and magnetism.
Field-line grammar carries over (tangent = direction, crowding = strength, no crossing) with one great exception: magnetic lines have no ends — they form closed loops.
No monopoles: every N is welded to an S; halving a magnet yields two dipoles. Poles are where loops enter/exit the material, not point sources.
Right-hand rule for a wire: thumb along the current, fingers curl the field circles; B = μ₀I/2πr around a straight wire (μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A) — quoted here, derived at Biot–Savart.
Canonical patterns: bar magnet (external loops N→S, closed through the body), straight wire (concentric circles), current loop (a face-threading field — a bar magnet in miniature).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Magnetic field lines start at the north pole and end at the south pole.
common_misconceptions.correction: They have no ends at all: outside the magnet they run N to S, but they continue THROUGH the magnet from S back to N, closing every loop. Unlike electric lines (which terminate on charges), magnetic lines cannot end — there are no magnetic charges to end on.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cutting a bar magnet in half separates the north pole from the south pole.
common_misconceptions.correction: You get two complete magnets, each with its own N and S — and halving again just makes more dipoles, down to the atomic scale. No experiment has ever isolated a monopole; poles are features of the loop pattern, not detachable objects.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Any charge creates a magnetic field, just as it creates an electric field.
common_misconceptions.correction: A charge at rest creates ONLY an electric field. Magnetic fields require MOTION — currents. That asymmetry is the domain's architecture: electrostatics from charge, magnetism from charge in motion (and, deeper, the two are one phenomenon viewed from different frames — a relativity-flavored footnote for later).

common_misconceptions.misconception: The magnetic field around a wire points away from the wire, like E from a charged line.
common_misconceptions.correction: B wraps AROUND the wire in closed circles — no component toward or away from it. The right-hand rule gives the circulation sense: thumb with the current, fingers curl with B. Radial is electric grammar; circulating is magnetic grammar.

visual_teaching_suggestions: The classic iron-filings triptych (real or simulated): bar magnet, straight wire, current loop — the three canonical patterns discovered rather than asserted.
A closed-loop revelation diagram: the bar magnet's field drawn INCLUDING the interior segment S→N, every line a complete loop — beside an electric dipole's terminating lines for contrast.
The magnet-cutting animation: halving yielding two dipoles, repeatedly, down to atomic current loops — the no-monopole rule made procedural.
A right-hand-rule practice widget: wires at random orientations, student curls a rendered hand, field circles appear with the correct sense.
A field-strength scale ladder (log): Earth 5 × 10⁻⁵ T, fridge magnet 10⁻² T, MRI 3 T, neutron star 10⁸ T — the tesla calibrated.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A long straight cable carries 30 A. (a) Using B = μ₀I/2πr, find the field strength 5.0 cm from the cable and compare it with Earth's field (~5.0 × 10⁻⁵ T). Would a compass held there be disturbed? (b) Sketch/describe the field pattern and give its direction on all sides for a current flowing north, using the right-hand rule. (c) At what distance from the cable does its field fall to one-tenth of Earth's?
steps: Comparison: 1.2 × 10⁻⁴/5.0 × 10⁻⁵ = 2.4 — the cable's field is about 2.4 times Earth's, so a compass 5 cm away would swing far from north (badly disturbed); household wiring genuinely confuses nearby compasses.

(b) The field is concentric circles around the cable. Right-hand rule with the current flowing north (thumb north): fingers curl so B points WEST above the cable, EAST below it, UP on the east side, DOWN on the west side — circulation, never radial.
Read the scaling: B ∝ 1/r (single power, not inverse square) — moving from 5 cm to 1.2 m (×24) cut the field ×24, from 2.4 Earths to a tenth of one.
Grammar audit: every direction answer was a TANGENT to a circle around the wire — any 'points away from the wire' answer fails the closed-loop rule on sight.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ T and judges the compass badly disturbed, assigns circulation directions on all four sides via the right-hand rule, inverts the formula to r = 1.2 m, and articulates the 1/r (not 1/r²) falloff and the circulating (not radial) geometry.

item_types: field-pattern identification and sketching (bar magnet, wire, loop — with direction labels)
right-hand-rule direction drills at varied orientations
B = μ₀I/2πr computations (solve for B, I, or r; compare with Earth's field)
conceptual items on closed loops, no monopoles, and the moving-charge source rule
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with pattern recognition and right-hand-rule drills, add single-wire field computations and Earth-field comparisons, then inverse solves and scaling questions, ending with conceptual contrasts against electric-field grammar (loops versus terminating lines, circulation versus radial).

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Understand — students must explain the field's sources, the closed-loop/no-monopole structure, and the canonical patterns with correct directions, beyond substituting into the wire formula.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student draws the three canonical patterns with correct closed-loop topology, applies the right-hand rule reliably, computes and compares field strengths at the 1/r scaling, and explains why motion is required to make B, at 75% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Navigation by compass — from ships to smartphones (magnetometers) — reads Earth's ~50 μT field, and must compensate for nearby currents and iron.
MRI machines image the body inside a 1.5–3 T field from superconducting coils — the tesla scale put to clinical work.
Electric motors and loudspeakers are current-and-field machines: every one is built on the field patterns of wires and loops drawn in this concept.
Maglev trains ride engineered magnetic-field geometry — levitation and guidance by shaped B.
Magnetic stripe readers, hard-disk heads, and security tags all write and read information as patterns of field — geometry of B as data.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to apply the Lorentz force F = q(E + v × B), compute the magnetic force magnitude qvB sin θ with direction from the right-hand rule, explain why magnetic forces do no work, analyze circular motion of charges in uniform fields (r = mv/qB, period independent of speed), and compute the force F = BIL on a current-carrying conductor.
assets.concept_summary: Now the magnetic field acts. The complete electromagnetic force on a charge is the LORENTZ FORCE, F = q(E + v × B): the electric part qE pushes along the field regardless of motion; the magnetic part qv × B is stranger in every way. Its magnitude is F = qvB sin θ — zero for a charge at rest (v = 0) and zero for motion parallel to the field (θ = 0), maximal when charge cuts across the field at right angles. Its direction is the cross product's: perpendicular to BOTH v and B, given by the right-hand rule (fingers point along v, curl toward B, thumb gives F for positive charge — reverse for negative). That perpendicularity has a profound consequence: the magnetic force NEVER does work. Always at right angles to the velocity, it can bend a trajectory but never speed it up or slow it down — kinetic energy is untouchable by B alone. The signature motion follows: a charge moving perpendicular to a uniform field is bent into a CIRCLE, the magnetic force playing centripetal: qvB = mv²/r gives radius r = mv/qB (fast or heavy → wide circle; strong field or big charge → tight one), and the period T = 2πm/qB is INDEPENDENT of speed — faster particles run bigger circles in exactly the same time, the resonance secret of the cyclotron. Velocity with a component along B adds an unbent drift: the path becomes a HELIX winding along the field line — the mechanism that funnels solar-wind particles along Earth's field toward the poles (auroras). Since currents are marching charges, a wire in a field feels the summed force: F = BIL sin θ on length L, direction again by the right-hand rule (fingers along conventional I) — the seed of every motor. Crossed E and B fields make a velocity selector (qE = qvB passes only v = E/B), and the same force physics runs mass spectrometers, cyclotrons, and the Hall effect.
key_ideas: Lorentz force: F = q(E + v × B) — the electric part acts always; the magnetic part only on MOVING charge, F = qvB sin θ.
Direction by right-hand rule (v curled into B, thumb = F for + charge; flip for −): always perpendicular to both v and B.
Magnetic forces do NO WORK: forever perpendicular to velocity, they bend paths but never change speed or kinetic energy.
Perpendicular entry → circular motion: qvB = mv²/r, so r = mv/qB; period T = 2πm/qB is speed-independent — the cyclotron's resonance principle.
Velocity along B is unaffected → helical paths winding along field lines (aurora funnel); crossed fields select speed v = E/B.
Current version: F = BIL sin θ on a straight wire (fingers along I) — charges' forces summed, and the working principle of motors.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The magnetic force pushes charges along the field lines, like the electric force does.
common_misconceptions.correction: The magnetic force is PERPENDICULAR to the field (and to the velocity) — a charge is never pushed along B by the magnetic force. Motion along B feels no force at all; the along-the-line picture belongs to E. Confusing the two grammars is the domain's most common wrong turn.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A magnetic field speeds particles up — that's how accelerators work.
common_misconceptions.correction: B alone cannot change a particle's speed: the force is always at right angles to the motion, so it does zero work. Cyclotrons BEND with the magnetic field and ACCELERATE with an electric field in the gaps — B steers, E feeds energy. Steering and speeding are different jobs.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A stationary charge near a magnet feels a magnetic force.
common_misconceptions.correction: No motion, no magnetic force: F = qvB sin θ vanishes at v = 0. A charge sitting beside the strongest MRI magnet feels nothing magnetic. (A moving OBSERVER complicates the story — a hint that E and B are frame-dependent faces of one field — but in your frame, static charge means zero magnetic force.)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Faster particles in a cyclotron take longer per revolution, since the circle is bigger.
common_misconceptions.correction: The period T = 2πm/qB contains no v: faster particles ride proportionally larger circles and complete them in exactly the same time. That speed-independence is precisely why a fixed-frequency alternating voltage stays synchronized — it is the design principle, not a coincidence (until relativity spoils it at near-light speeds).

visual_teaching_suggestions: A right-hand-rule trainer: randomized v and B arrows, student orients a rendered hand, F appears — with negative-charge reversal drills.
The no-work animation: a charge circling in uniform B with speed and kinetic-energy meters pinned constant while the velocity ARROW rotates.

A circular-to-helical morph: perpendicular entry giving a circle, then a growing parallel velocity component stretching it into a helix along the field line — ending with the aurora funnel at Earth's poles.
A cyclotron schematic in motion: two dees, B bending the path, E kicking at each gap crossing, radius growing while the lap time stays fixed.
The F = BIL jumping-wire demo (or video): a rod on rails in a magnet gap leaping when current flows, direction flipping with current or field reversal.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A proton (m = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) enters a uniform field B = 0.50 T at v = 2.0 × 10⁶ m/s, perpendicular to B. (a) Find the magnetic force on it. (b) Find the radius of its circular path and its period. (c) How much work does the field do per revolution? (d) A wire 0.20 m long carrying 15 A sits perpendicular to the same field — find the force on it.
steps: (a) F = qvB sin 90° = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 2.0 × 10⁶ × 0.50 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N — tiny in newtons, but colossal against the proton's weight (~1.6 × 10⁻²⁶ N): thirteen orders of magnitude; gravity is irrelevant to particle optics.
Period: T = 2πm/qB = (2π × 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷)/8.0 × 10⁻²⁰ = 1.05 × 10⁻²⁶/8.0 × 10⁻²⁰ ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻⁷ s — and note v appears nowhere: a proton at twice the speed rides a 8.4 cm circle in the same 0.13 μs.
(c) Zero. The force is perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, so W = 0 over any path segment and any number of revolutions — the speed stays 2.0 × 10⁶ m/s forever (in B alone).
(d) F = BIL sin 90° = 0.50 × 15 × 0.20 = 1.5 N — a full, hand-feelable newton and a half: summed over ~10²³ drifting electrons, the same force that was 10⁻¹³ N per particle runs motors.

Direction discipline: each force answer requires the right-hand rule with the actual geometry; for the wire, fingers follow CONVENTIONAL current. Every F must come out perpendicular to both its v (or I) and B — any answer along B fails on sight.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes F = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ N, r ≈ 4.2 cm, and T ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻⁷ s, states the speed-independence of T, answers zero work with the perpendicularity argument, and finds the 1.5 N wire force — applying the right-hand rule consistently throughout.

item_types: force computations F = qvB sin θ and F = BIL sin θ with right-hand-rule directions
circular-motion problems (r, T, frequency; solve for m, q, or B — mass-spectrometer style)
no-work and energy-invariance reasoning items
combined-field problems (velocity selector qE = qvB, helical paths)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with magnitude computations and direction drills, add circular-motion solves in both directions, then work/energy reasoning and speed-independent-period questions, ending with velocity selectors, helices, and mass-spectrometer compositions.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must apply the Lorentz force in magnitude and direction to particle and wire scenarios, and use the circular-motion relations quantitatively, beyond stating F = qv × B.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student computes forces with correct right-hand-rule directions (including negative charges), solves r = mv/qB problems both ways, defends the zero-work property, and handles selector/helix compositions, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Mass spectrometers identify molecules by r = mv/qB: same speed in, each mass bends onto its own radius — chemistry, forensics, and Mars rovers all carry one.
Cyclotrons and synchrotrons steer particle beams with B and feed them energy with E — medical isotope production and the LHC alike.
Every electric motor is F = BIL harnessed: current-carrying loops forced to turn in a field.
The aurora is Lorentz-force choreography: solar-wind charges spiral helically along Earth's field lines into the polar atmosphere.
Hall-effect sensors (in phones, cars, brushless motors) read the sideways qv × B push on carriers as a voltage — the force turned into a measurement.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to state the Biot–Savart law dB = (μ₀/4π)(I dl × r̂)/r² as the field contribution of a current element, explain its structure (inverse-square, perpendicular geometry, superposition), apply its standard results — the circular-loop center B = μ₀I/2R (and N-turn coils), the long straight wire B = μ₀I/2πr — and choose between them by geometry.
assets.concept_summary: Biot–Savart is magnetism's Coulomb law: it prices the field made by each infinitesimal piece of a current. A current element I dl (a short arrow of wire carrying current I) contributes dB = (μ₀/4π)(I dl × r̂)/r² at a point distance r away — and every clause of the formula teaches. The 1/r² is the familiar inverse square of point sources. The current element I dl replaces the point charge q — magnetism's source is not charge but charge IN MOTION, dealt out along the wire. The CROSS PRODUCT dl × r̂ encodes the strange geometry: the contribution is perpendicular to both the element and the line to the field point, strongest broadside to the element (sin θ = 1) and exactly ZERO straight ahead of or behind it (a current element throws no field along its own axis). The constant μ₀/4π = 10⁻⁷ T·m/A makes the numbers honest. No lone current element exists — currents flow in closed circuits — so the law is used by SUPERPOSITION: integrate the contributions around the actual wire. Two integrals do most of physics' daily work, and both should be owned as results with their logic. CENTER OF A CIRCULAR LOOP: every element is perpendicular to the line to the center at the same distance R, so contributions simply pile up: B = μ₀I/2R, multiplied to B = μ₀NI/2R for an N-turn coil — the field-concentration trick behind every electromagnet coil. LONG STRAIGHT WIRE: the full integral delivers B = μ₀I/2πr — the concentric-circle field quoted back at magnetic-field, now derived, its single power of 1/r explained (contributions from an infinite line accumulate, softening the point-source 1/r² to 1/r — the same geometry-driven softening seen for the infinite charged line in electrostatics). Comparing the two results at equal distance (loop center versus wire at r = R) gives B_loop/B_wire = π: curling the wire around the point concentrates its field π-fold. Biot–Savart handles ANY geometry by honest integration (arcs contribute in proportion to their angle; straight segments need the full treatment), but for high-symmetry cases a shortcut is coming — Ampère's law is to Biot–Savart what Gauss was to Coulomb.
key_ideas: dB = (μ₀/4π)(I dl × r̂)/r²: each current element contributes an inverse-square, cross-product field — magnetism's Coulomb law, sourced by moving charge.
Geometry clause: dB ⊥ both dl and r̂, maximal broadside (sin θ = 1), ZERO along the element's own axis — the cross product is the physics, not decoration.
μ₀/4π = 10⁻⁷ T·m/A exactly — the magnetic constant in its most computable form.
Fields of real circuits come by superposition: integrate dB around the closed wire — no isolated current element exists.
Standard result 1 — loop center: B = μ₀I/2R (all elements broadside and equidistant, contributions aligned); N turns multiply: B = μ₀NI/2R.

Standard result 2 — long straight wire: B = μ₀I/2πr derived; the 1/r (not 1/r²) falloff is the infinite-line accumulation, and the loop-versus-wire comparison gives the π-fold concentration of curling the current around the point.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A current element creates field all around it equally, like a point charge does.
common_misconceptions.correction: The cross product kills the field along the element's axis: dB ∝ sin θ, zero straight ahead and behind, maximal broadside. A current element is a directional lamp, not a bare bulb — and summing these directional contributions is why circuit geometry matters so much.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The straight wire's B = μ₀I/2πr falls as 1/r, so Biot–Savart must not really be inverse-square.
common_misconceptions.correction: Each ELEMENT contributes as 1/r² exactly; the 1/r of the whole wire is what an infinite line of inverse-square contributions integrates to — nearer the wire, more of it matters. Same resolution as the charged line in electrostatics: the law is about elements, the falloff of a TOTAL depends on the source's shape.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Since the loop-center formula is B = μ₀I/2R, a bigger loop gives a bigger field.
common_misconceptions.correction: R is in the DENOMINATOR: a bigger loop puts every element farther from the center and the field is WEAKER. Field concentration wants small, many-turned coils (B = μ₀NI/2R) — small R and large N is the electromagnet recipe.

common_misconceptions.misconception: You must redo the full integral for every problem.
common_misconceptions.correction: The standard results are reusable tools: loop center μ₀I/2R (N turns: ×N), straight wire μ₀I/2πr, arcs in proportion to their angle (a half-loop gives half of μ₀I/2R). Recognize the geometry, deploy the result, and reserve honest integration for genuinely new shapes.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A current-element 'directional lamp' rendering: dB magnitude drawn over all directions around one element — broadside maximum, dead zones fore and aft along its axis.
The loop-center superposition animation: contributions from elements around the ring, each perpendicular and equal, stacking into the axial B = μ₀I/2R.
The straight-wire integral pictured: elements along the line, near ones contributing strongly, far ones weakly, the running sum converging to μ₀I/2πr.
A loop-versus-wire comparison graphic at the same distance: the π-fold concentration of curling the current around the point, with both numbers computed.
An arc calculator: fields at the center of quarter-, half-, and full loops (proportional to angle), composed with straight-segment feeds — the exam-problem toolkit visualized.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A circular coil of radius R = 0.10 m carries I = 5.0 A. (a) Find the field at its center for a single turn. (b) Compare with the field 0.10 m from a long straight wire carrying the same current, and explain the ratio. (c) The coil is rewound with N = 100 turns at the same radius — find the new central field. (d) What field would a half-loop (semicircle) of the same radius and current produce at the center?
steps: (a) B = μ₀I/2R = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 5.0)/(2 × 0.10) = (6.28 × 10⁻⁶)/0.20 = 3.1 × 10⁻⁵ T — a single turn at 10 cm rivals Earth's field.

Why π: curling the wire into a loop wraps the current around the point so every element sits broadside (sin θ = 1) at the minimum distance — the circle concentrates what the straight line spreads; the ratio is exactly (μ₀I/2R)/(μ₀I/2πR) = π.
(c) N aligned turns superpose: B = μ₀NI/2R = 100 × 3.1 × 10⁻⁵ = 3.1 × 10⁻³ T — a hundredfold for the cost of winding: the electromagnet's core trick.

(d) An arc contributes in proportion to its angle: a semicircle gives half the full loop, B = ½ × μ₀I/2R = μ₀I/4R = 1.6 × 10⁻⁵ T. (The straight feed wires, if they run radially through the center, contribute ZERO there — dl × r̂ vanishes along their own line: the dead-zone clause earning its keep.)
Toolkit audit: three reusable results (loop, wire, arc-fraction) plus one structural fact (no field along an element's axis) solved every part — no integral was recomputed.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes 3.1 × 10⁻⁵ T for the loop center, explains the π ratio against the wire's 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ T geometrically, scales to 3.1 mT for 100 turns, and halves the loop result for the semicircle while zeroing the radial feeds by the axis dead-zone rule.

item_types: standard-result computations (loop center, N-turn coils, straight wire) solving for B, I, R, or N
composite-geometry problems (arcs + straight segments, concentric loops, opposed currents)
structure-of-the-law items (dead zones, broadside maxima, why 1/r for the wire)
comparison and scaling questions (loop versus wire, radius and turn-count scaling)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with single-formula plug-ins, add N-turn and arc-fraction variations, then composite center-field problems with zero-contribution feeds, ending with structural explanations (cross-product geometry, integral origins of 1/r) and multi-source superpositions.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must deploy the Biot–Savart standard results across geometries and compose arc/segment fields, beyond quoting the differential law.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student computes loop, coil, and wire fields with correct scaling, composes arc-plus-segment geometries including zero-axis contributions, and explains the law's cross-product structure and the wire's 1/r origin, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Electromagnet and inductor design starts at B = μ₀NI/2R — turn count and radius are the engineer's levers for field per ampere.
MRI gradient coils are Biot–Savart engineering: precisely shaped windings sculpt the spatial field profile that encodes position in the image.
Magnetic stimulation therapy (TMS) computes cortical fields from coil geometry by Biot–Savart before any pulse is delivered.
Beam-steering magnets in accelerators and electron microscopes are designed by integrating current geometries to shape the bending field.
Compass deviation compensation on ships and aircraft models the fields of onboard wiring runs — Biot–Savart audits of every current path near the sensor.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to state Ampère's circuital law ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc, explain circulation and enclosed current, apply it with symmetry-matched Amperian loops to derive the fields of a long straight wire (inside and outside a thick conductor), recognize when the law applies usefully, and connect it to Gauss's-law-style reasoning.
assets.concept_summary: Ampère's law is to Biot–Savart what Gauss's law was to Coulomb: the same physics repackaged as a global statement that turns symmetric problems into one-line derivations. The statement: the CIRCULATION of the magnetic field around any closed loop — ∮B·dl, the running sum of B's along-the-path component times path length — equals μ₀ times the total current THREADING the loop: ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc. Every structural clause echoes Gauss with the geometry rotated: where Gauss used FLUX through a closed SURFACE counting enclosed CHARGE, Ampère uses CIRCULATION around a closed CURVE counting enclosed (threading) CURRENT — the swap reflects the fields' natures, E diverging from charges, B circulating around currents. Only currents piercing the loop's spanned surface count; currents outside contribute circulation zero (their positive and negative path segments cancel exactly), and the law holds for ANY loop — but is USEFUL only when symmetry lets B be pulled out of the integral. The working method mirrors Gauss's three steps: (1) read the symmetry to know B's shape (circles around a straight wire, uniform along a solenoid's axis); (2) choose an Amperian loop on which B is constant-and-parallel (a concentric circle; a rectangle half-in half-out); (3) then ∮B·dl collapses to B × (path length) and B follows arithmetically. The showcase is the THICK WIRE of radius a carrying uniform current I: outside (r > a), a concentric circular loop encloses all of I, so B·2πr = μ₀I gives B = μ₀I/2πr — the whole wire acts as if concentrated on its axis (shell-theorem flavor, magnetically); inside (r < a), the loop encloses only the fraction I(r²/a²) that threads it, so B = μ₀Ir/2πa² — rising LINEARLY from zero at the center, peaking at the surface, then handing over to the 1/r exterior. One asterisk belongs in every honest statement: the law as written holds for STEADY currents; changing electric fields add a term (Maxwell's displacement current) — the amendment that completes the law is deferred to Maxwell's equations, where it becomes the key to light itself.
key_ideas: ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc: the circulation of B around any closed loop equals μ₀ times the current threading it — Ampère's circuital law.
Gauss-with-geometry-rotated: flux/surface/charge becomes circulation/curve/current — E diverges from its sources, B circulates around them.
Only THREADING currents count: currents outside the loop circulate net zero; the law is always true, but only USEFUL when symmetry tames the integral.
Method: read symmetry → choose an Amperian loop where B is constant and parallel to the path → circulation collapses to B × length → solve.
Showcase (thick wire, radius a): outside B = μ₀I/2πr (all current enclosed — axis-equivalent); inside B = μ₀Ir/2πa² (only the r²/a² fraction threads) — linear rise, surface peak, 1/r fall.
Fine print: valid for steady currents; changing E-fields add Maxwell's displacement-current term — the completion deferred to Maxwell's equations.

common_misconceptions.misconception: If a current sits outside the Amperian loop, the field on the loop is zero.
common_misconceptions.correction: The FIELD on the loop is generally nonzero — the outside current's B is right there. What vanishes is its net CIRCULATION: along-the-path contributions cancel around the closed curve. Ampère's law counts enclosed current only, but says nothing about B being zero pointwise; conflating circulation with field is the central trap.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Ampère's law only works for special loops, so it must be an approximation.
common_misconceptions.correction: The law is EXACT for every closed loop (steady currents) — as exact as Gauss's law. What's special about symmetric loops is only that they make the integral SOLVABLE by hand: B constant-and-parallel collapses ∮B·dl to B × length. Loop choice is strategy, not validity.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Inside a current-carrying wire the field is zero, like inside the charged conductor of electrostatics.
common_misconceptions.correction: The electrostatic zero was for STATIC charge on a conductor. A wire CARRYING current is not electrostatic — current fills its cross-section, and the interior field grows linearly, B = μ₀Ir/2πa², from zero at the axis to a maximum at the surface. Enclosed current, not conductor-ness, decides.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Ampère's law and Biot–Savart can disagree, since they look so different.
common_misconceptions.correction: They are the same physics: Ampère's law follows from Biot–Savart for steady currents, exactly as Gauss follows from Coulomb. Any geometry solvable by both gives identical answers (the straight wire's μ₀I/2πr checks). Choose by convenience — symmetry favors Ampère; arbitrary shapes need Biot–Savart.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A side-by-side Gauss/Ampère dictionary table-graphic: closed surface ↔ closed loop, flux ↔ circulation, Q_enc ↔ I_enc, 1/ε₀ ↔ μ₀ — the analogy as one picture.
A circulation-meter animation: a bead walked around loops enclosing and not enclosing a wire, the running ∮B·dl accumulating to μ₀I or exactly zero.
The thick-wire field profile: B(r) plotted rising linearly to the surface then falling as 1/r, with the two Amperian circles (inside/outside) drawn beneath their segments of the curve.
An enclosed-current counter: loops threading one, two, or zero of several wires (with signs by direction), the circulation updating with each.
The loop-choice gallery: good Amperian loops (concentric circle, solenoid rectangle) versus useless ones (tilted ellipse) — validity everywhere, usefulness only with symmetry.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A long straight conductor of radius a = 2.0 mm carries I = 10 A distributed uniformly over its cross-section. Find the magnetic field (a) at r = 4.0 mm from the axis, (b) at r = 1.0 mm (inside), and (c) at the surface r = a. (d) Sketch B(r) and state where the field is maximum.
steps: (a) Outside: a concentric Amperian circle of radius 4.0 mm encloses the full 10 A. Symmetry: B is constant and tangent on the circle, so ∮B·dl = B·2πr = μ₀I → B = μ₀I/2πr = (2 × 10⁻⁷ × 10)/0.0040 = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ T.
(b) Inside: the loop at r = 1.0 mm encloses only the current threading it: I_enc = I(r²/a²) = 10 × (1.0/2.0)² = 2.5 A.

B·2πr = μ₀I_enc → B = (2 × 10⁻⁷ × 2.5)/0.0010 = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ T — numerically the same as at 4.0 mm, a neat coincidence of this problem (r = a/2 and r = 2a give equal B, since linear rise and 1/r fall mirror about the peak).
(c) At the surface both formulas agree (continuity check): B = μ₀I/2πa = (2 × 10⁻⁷ × 10)/0.0020 = 1.0 × 10⁻³ T — the maximum.
(d) B(r) rises linearly from 0 at the axis to 1.0 mT at r = a, then falls as 1/r outside — the tent-shaped profile with its peak at the surface; the interior linearity comes directly from I_enc ∝ r².

Method audit: every part was three lines — symmetry (circles), loop choice (concentric), collapse (B·2πr = μ₀I_enc). Biot–Savart would integrate for a page to say the same; that economy is the law's entire job.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes 0.50 mT at both 4.0 mm and 1.0 mm (recognizing the enclosed-fraction logic inside), finds the 1.0 mT surface maximum with both formulas agreeing, and sketches the linear-rise/1-over-r-fall profile, crediting the symmetry method for the brevity.

item_types: Amperian-loop applications (thick wires inside/outside, coaxial cables, current sheets)
enclosed-current bookkeeping (multiple wires, signed threading, partial enclosure)
circulation-versus-field conceptual items (outside currents, loop validity versus usefulness)
profile-sketching and continuity checks (B(r) across boundaries)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with single-wire outside/inside computations, add enclosed-fraction and multi-wire sign bookkeeping, then coaxial-cable regional analyses, ending with conceptual circulation/field distinctions and the steady-current fine print.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must apply the circulation law with symmetry-matched loops across regions and count threading currents correctly, beyond stating ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student derives inside/outside fields with correct enclosed-current fractions, maintains continuity at boundaries, distinguishes circulation from pointwise field for external currents, and states the steady-current limitation, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Coaxial cables are Ampère's law engineered: equal and opposite currents on core and shield make the external circulation — and field — zero, which is why coax doesn't radiate into (or hear) its neighbors.
Current transformers and clamp meters measure a wire's current from the circulation around it without touching it — Ampère's law as an instrument.
Busbar and conductor design uses the interior linear field profile to compute forces and inductance within thick conductors.
Tokamak and toroidal-confinement physics rests on Ampère's-law field bookkeeping of plasma and coil currents.
EMC (electromagnetic compatibility) practice — twisted pairs, return-path routing — is applied enclosed-current cancellation: minimize net threading current, minimize stray field.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to derive the solenoid's interior field B = μ₀nI by Ampère's law, distinguish turn density n = N/L from total turns, describe the ideal solenoid's uniform-inside/zero-outside field, apply the toroid result B = μ₀NI/2πr, and reason about real-world deviations (end effects, finite length).
assets.concept_summary: The solenoid is the answer to a design brief: build a UNIFORM magnetic field on demand. Wind a long wire into a tight helix of N turns over length L (turn density n = N/L, turns per metre) and the geometry performs a beautiful cancellation-and-reinforcement: between neighboring turns the fields oppose and cancel; inside the bore every turn's contribution aligns AXIALLY, stacking into a strong, straight, uniform field; outside, the return field spreads over so much space that (for the ideal infinite solenoid) it vanishes. Ampère's law harvests the result in three lines: take a rectangular Amperian loop straddling the winding, one side inside (parallel to the axis, length ℓ), one side outside (B = 0), two sides perpendicular (no along-path contribution) — circulation = Bℓ; the threading current is nℓ turns each carrying I, so Bℓ = μ₀nℓI, and B = μ₀nI. The formula's austerity is the lesson: the interior field depends ONLY on turn density and current — not on the solenoid's radius, not on position inside (uniform!), not on total length. Doubling the turns per metre or the current doubles B; a fatter bore gives the same field over more volume. Real solenoids honor the ideal in the middle and betray it at the ends: the field sags to about HALF the central value at each opening (end effect), and 'long compared to its radius' is the fidelity condition — a length-to-radius ratio of 10 keeps the central region uniform to fine precision. The TOROID is the solenoid bent into a doughnut, swallowing its own ends: now the field is confined ENTIRELY within the core, circulating along it with B = μ₀NI/2πr (from a circular Amperian loop of radius r inside the core — note it thins slightly toward the outer edge as 1/r, and is exactly zero both in the hole and outside the body). No ends, no leakage: the toroid is the choice where stray field is intolerable (transformers, tokamaks). Add a ferromagnetic core to either and the field multiplies enormously — the electromagnet — but that story belongs to magnetic materials.
key_ideas: Solenoid interior: B = μ₀nI with n = N/L turns per metre — uniform, axial, and independent of radius and of position inside.
The geometry's trick: between-turn fields cancel, axial contributions stack — uniform inside, (ideally) zero outside; the return flux spreads to nothing.
Ampère derivation in three lines: rectangular loop half-in half-out → circulation Bℓ, threading current nℓI → B = μ₀nI.
Turn DENSITY, not turn count: 1000 turns on 10 cm beats 1000 turns on 1 m tenfold — n is the design lever, with I.
Real solenoids: field ≈ half the central value at the ends; ideality needs length ≫ radius (L/R ≳ 10 for a clean central region).
Toroid — the solenoid that ate its ends: B = μ₀NI/2πr inside the core only (thinning as 1/r across it), zero in the hole and outside: total field confinement.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A wider solenoid produces a weaker field, since the same flux spreads over more area.
common_misconceptions.correction: B = μ₀nI contains no radius: the interior field is set by turn density and current alone. A fatter bore delivers the SAME field strength filling MORE volume (more flux, same B). Radius costs wire and power, not field strength.

common_misconceptions.misconception: More total turns always means a stronger field.
common_misconceptions.correction: Only turns per METRE matter: n = N/L. Stretching 1000 turns from 10 cm to 1 m dilutes n tenfold and the field with it. Winding density — turns packed per unit length — is the lever; total count without length is meaningless.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The field just inside the end of a solenoid equals the central value μ₀nI.
common_misconceptions.correction: At the ends the field drops to about HALF the central value — the missing 'other half' of the infinite solenoid no longer contributes. Uniformity is a deep-interior property; instruments needing clean fields work in the middle third and treat the ends as fringe country.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A toroid's field fills the doughnut hole and leaks outside, like a bar magnet's.
common_misconceptions.correction: An ideal toroid confines its field ENTIRELY within the winding's core: Amperian loops in the hole enclose no current, and loops outside enclose equal and opposite threading (net zero). Inside the core B = μ₀NI/2πr; everywhere else, zero — that confinement is the toroid's whole point.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A turn-stacking animation: single loop's field, then 5, 20, 100 turns compounding — between-turn cancellation and axial reinforcement building the uniform bore field.
The Ampère rectangle diagram: the half-in half-out loop over a cutaway solenoid, each side's contribution labelled (Bℓ, 0, 0, 0), the three-line derivation beside it.
A field-map comparison: ideal solenoid (uniform inside, zero outside) versus a real finite one (bulging fringe fields, half-strength ends) — same colormap.
The solenoid-to-toroid morph: bending the cylinder until its ends meet, the fringe fields swallowed, the field now circulating confined in the core with the 1/r shading across it.
A design-lever dashboard: sliders for N, L, I (and radius, showing NO effect on B) with the bore field and required wire length updating — n as the real variable.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A solenoid 0.50 m long is wound with 2000 turns and carries 3.0 A. (a) Find the interior field. (b) The same 2000 turns are rewound over 1.0 m — what is the field now? (c) What current would restore the original field in the stretched winding? (d) A toroid with 2000 turns and mean radius 0.25 m carries the same 3.0 A — find the field at the mean radius and state the field in the doughnut's hole.
steps: (a) n = N/L = 2000/0.50 = 4000 turns/m. B = μ₀nI = 4π × 10⁻⁷ × 4000 × 3.0 = 1.5 × 10⁻² T = 15 mT — a respectable laboratory field from bench-top numbers.
(b) Stretched: n = 2000/1.0 = 2000 /m → B = μ₀nI = 4π × 10⁻⁷ × 2000 × 3.0 = 7.5 mT — HALVED, though not one turn was removed: density, not count, is the lever.
(c) Restore 15 mT: with n halved, I must double → I = 6.0 A. (Cost note: doubling I quadruples the winding's I²R heat — density bought with copper beats field bought with current.)
In the hole (and outside the body): exactly ZERO — Amperian loops there enclose no net current; the toroid's field lives only within its core.

Consistency check: the toroid formula is the solenoid's in disguise — its effective turn density is n = N/2πr = 2000/1.571 ≈ 1273 /m, and μ₀nI = 4π × 10⁻⁷ × 1273 × 3.0 ≈ 4.8 mT ✓ — the bent solenoid remembers its origin.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes 15 mT, halves it to 7.5 mT under stretching (articulating density-not-count), restores it with 6.0 A noting the I²R cost, and finds 4.8 mT in the toroid core with zero in the hole — verifying the toroid as a closed solenoid.

item_types: B = μ₀nI computations (solve for B, n, I, or N/L; rewinding variations)
toroid problems (mean-radius fields, inner/outer variation, hole and exterior zeros)
derivation and justification items (the Ampère rectangle, why radius is absent)
design problems (specify n and I for a target field; end-effect and L/R fidelity reasoning)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with direct B = μ₀nI plug-ins and rewinding contrasts, add toroid computations with region reasoning, then the Ampère-loop derivation reproduced, ending with design-to-specification and end-effect/fidelity questions.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Apply — students must apply B = μ₀nI and the toroid formula across rewinding, design, and regional-field scenarios, and reproduce the Amperian derivation, beyond memorizing the formulas.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student computes solenoid and toroid fields with the density-versus-count distinction intact, derives B = μ₀nI from the rectangular loop, assigns zero field correctly to toroid hole/exterior, and designs windings to specification, at 80% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: MRI magnets are superconducting solenoids: a human-bore helix carrying persistent current makes the 1.5–3 T uniform field the imaging depends on.
Every relay, contactor, and car-starter solenoid is B = μ₀nI put to work — current in, plunger pulled, circuit switched.
Inductors and transformer windings are solenoids and toroids by trade; toroidal cores are chosen exactly for their field confinement (low interference).
Tokamak fusion reactors confine plasma in toroidal fields — the zero-leakage doughnut scaled to megamperes.
Particle-beam and electron-microscope focusing coils use solenoid fields as magnetic lenses — uniformity by winding density, aim by geometry.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to classify materials as diamagnetic, paramagnetic, or ferromagnetic by their response to external fields, explain each behavior via atomic magnetic moments and domains, use relative permeability μr to quantify the response, describe hysteresis and the Curie temperature, and predict core effects on solenoid fields.
assets.concept_summary: Put matter into a magnetic field and the matter answers back — in one of three voices. The atomic origin is current at the smallest scale: orbiting and spinning electrons are microscopic current loops, each a tiny magnetic moment; how a material's electron moments are arranged (or absent) and how they respond decides its class. DIAMAGNETIC materials (bismuth, copper, water, most organic matter) have NO permanent atomic moments: the applied field induces feeble opposing loops (an atomic-scale Lenz effect), so they are WEAKLY REPELLED — relative permeability μr a hair below 1 (bismuth 0.99983), effect so faint it hides beneath anything stronger, yet universal (every substance is diamagnetic underneath) and, in extremis, capable of levitating a frog in a 16 T bore. PARAMAGNETIC materials (aluminium, sodium, oxygen) have permanent atomic moments that are randomized by thermal jostling: an applied field partially aligns them, giving weak ATTRACTION — μr a hair above 1 (aluminium 1.00002), strengthening as temperature falls (less jostling — Curie's law) . FERROMAGNETIC materials — iron, nickel, cobalt, and their alloys — are the loud exception: quantum exchange coupling locks NEIGHBORING moments parallel across whole regions called DOMAINS (each a microscopic magnet, ordinarily pointing every which way so the bulk shows nothing); an applied field grows favorably-aligned domains and swings others into line, multiplying the field THOUSANDS-fold — μr of order 10²–10⁵, the reason iron cores turn modest solenoids into industrial electromagnets (B = μrμ₀nI). Ferromagnetism carries two signature behaviors: HYSTERESIS — the domain realignment doesn't fully undo when the field is removed, leaving remanent magnetization (memory: permanent magnets, tape, hard disks; the B–H loop's area is the energy lost per cycle, so transformer cores want narrow loops, magnets fat ones) — and the CURIE TEMPERATURE, above which thermal agitation defeats the exchange coupling and iron abruptly resigns to mere paramagnetism (770 °C for iron; a heated magnet drops its keys). Three voices, one physics: atomic moments, and whether they exist, merely lean, or lock arms.
key_ideas: Atomic origin: electron orbits and spins are microscopic current loops with magnetic moments — a material's class is set by whether moments exist and how they respond.
Diamagnetic: no permanent moments; induced opposing loops → weak REPULSION, μr slightly < 1 — universal, faint, the substrate beneath all other magnetism.
Paramagnetic: permanent moments randomized by heat, partially aligned by the field → weak ATTRACTION, μr slightly > 1, stronger when cold (Curie's law).
Ferromagnetic: exchange coupling locks neighboring moments into DOMAINS; the field grows and aligns domains → μr ~ 10²–10⁵, thousands-fold amplification (B = μrμ₀nI with a core).

Hysteresis: domain alignment persists after the field is gone — remanence makes permanent magnets and data storage; B–H loop area = energy lost per cycle (narrow loops for transformers, fat for magnets).
Curie temperature: above it thermal agitation breaks the exchange lock and ferromagnets fall to paramagnetism (iron: 770 °C) — magnetic order melts before the metal does.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All materials are attracted to magnets — some just too weakly to notice.
common_misconceptions.correction: Diamagnetic materials are REPELLED — water, copper, bismuth, and you. The effect is feeble but real (strong fields levitate frogs and graphite). The three classes differ in SIGN as well as size: repelled (dia), weakly attracted (para), fiercely attracted (ferro).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Iron is magnetic because its atoms are magnetic, and other metals' atoms aren't.
common_misconceptions.correction: Aluminium atoms carry magnetic moments too — iron's difference is COOPERATION: exchange coupling locks neighboring moments parallel into domains. Ferromagnetism is a collective, solid-state phenomenon, not an atomic one — the same iron atoms above 770 °C (Curie point) are merely paramagnetic.

common_misconceptions.misconception: An unmagnetized iron nail contains no magnetism until a magnet 'puts some in'.
common_misconceptions.correction: The nail is FULL of magnetism — countless domains, each saturated — but they point randomly and cancel. Magnetizing is reorganization, not injection: the external field grows aligned domains at their neighbors' expense. That's also why hammering or heating (disordering the domains) demagnetizes.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hysteresis is a defect that better materials avoid entirely.
common_misconceptions.correction: Hysteresis is a TOOL as much as a tax: remanence IS the permanent magnet and IS magnetic data storage. Engineers choose the loop for the job — soft magnetic materials (narrow loop, low loss) for transformer cores, hard ones (fat loop, stubborn remanence) for magnets and memory.

visual_teaching_suggestions: A three-panel response chart: dia/para/ferro samples near a strong magnet — feeble repulsion, feeble attraction, violent attraction — with μr values (0.99983, 1.00002, ~5000) captioned.
A domain animation: randomly oriented domains in unmagnetized iron, an applied field growing aligned domains and rotating others, then partial persistence after field removal (remanence).
The B–H hysteresis loop, annotated: initial magnetization curve, saturation, remanence, coercivity — with a narrow (transformer) and fat (permanent-magnet) loop overlaid.
A Curie-point demonstration storyboard: iron pendant clinging to a magnet, heated past 770 °C, dropping — order melting on camera.
A solenoid-core comparison: the same coil's field with air, then iron core, the B readout multiplying by μr — the electromagnet born in one substitution.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A solenoid with 1000 turns per metre carries 2.0 A. (a) Find its interior field with an air core. (b) An iron core of relative permeability μr = 400 is inserted — find the new field. (c) Classify these observations: bismuth (μr = 0.99983) is pushed weakly out of a strong field; aluminium (μr = 1.00002) is drawn weakly in; the iron core multiplied the field 400-fold. (d) The iron is heated above 770 °C — what happens to the field in the solenoid, and why?
steps: (b) Iron core: B = μrμ₀nI = 400 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 1.0 T — the same coil and current now rival an MRI magnet: the multiplication is entirely the core's domain alignment.

(c) Bismuth: repelled, μr < 1 → DIAMAGNETIC (induced opposing moments, no permanent ones). Aluminium: weakly attracted, μr barely > 1 → PARAMAGNETIC (permanent moments, thermally randomized, faintly aligned). Iron: μr ~ 10² → FERROMAGNETIC (domain cooperation).

Scale reading: dia and para deviate from μr = 1 in the 4th–5th decimal; ferro deviates by a factor of hundreds — the classes are separated by SIX orders of magnitude of response, not shades of the same effect.

(d) Above the Curie temperature the exchange coupling loses to thermal agitation: domains dissolve, iron becomes merely paramagnetic (μr ≈ 1), and the field collapses from 1.0 T back to essentially the air-core 2.5 mT.
Moral audit: the coil supplied 2.5 mT of 'seed' field throughout; the missing 0.9975 T was matter's contribution — magnetism borrowed from electron alignment, repayable on heating.
worked_example_blueprint.expected_outcome: The student computes 2.5 mT air-core and 1.0 T iron-core fields, classifies the three materials from sign and size of response, and predicts the collapse to ~2.5 mT above the Curie point with the domain-dissolution mechanism stated.

item_types: classification items (from μr values, force sign, or described behavior)
core-multiplication computations (B = μrμ₀nI, solve for any factor)
mechanism explanations (induced loops, thermal randomization, domains, Curie transition)
hysteresis-loop reading and material-selection questions (soft versus hard)
practice_blueprint.difficulty_progression: Begin with three-way classification from data, add core-field computations, then mechanism explanations at the atomic/domain level, ending with hysteresis-loop interpretation and application-matched material selection.

assessment_blueprint.bloom_alignment: Understand — students must explain the three response mechanisms and their signatures (μr, hysteresis, Curie point), beyond labelling materials.
assessment_blueprint.mastery_signal: The student classifies materials from behavior or μr, computes core-multiplied fields, explains domains/remanence/Curie transition mechanistically, and matches loop shapes to applications, at 75% accuracy or better.

real_world_applications: Transformer and motor cores are soft ferromagnets (silicon steel, ferrites): μr in the thousands multiplies flux, narrow hysteresis loops keep the per-cycle tax small.
Permanent magnets (neodymium, alnico) are engineered remanence — fat hysteresis loops that hold their alignment against everything short of heat and hammer.
Magnetic data storage — hard disks, tape, stripe cards — writes bits as local remanent magnetization: hysteresis as memory.
MRI exploits paramagnetic contrast agents (gadolinium) and the diamagnetism of tissue water — all three classes appear in one scan.
Induction cooktops need ferromagnetic pans: the pan's domains and eddy losses turn field oscillations into dinner — copper and aluminium pans stay cold.

assets.learning_objective: Students will be able to define the magnetic dipole moment m = NIA (direction by right-hand rule), compute the torque τ = mB sin θ and potential energy U = −mB cos θ of a dipole in a uniform field, explain compass alignment and motor action, and describe Earth's magnetism as a giant tilted dipole with declination and inclination.
assets.concept_summary: The magnetic dipole is the domain's atom of magnetism — the smallest complete magnetic identity, and the exact twin of the electric dipole met in electrostatics. A flat coil of N turns, area A, carrying current I has magnetic dipole moment m = NIA (units A·m²), directed along the coil's axis by the right-hand rule (curl fingers with the current; thumb is m). The moment is the coil's handle: a uniform field grips it with TORQUE τ = mB sin θ — zero when m aligns with B, maximal broadside — twisting the dipole toward alignment exactly as the electric field twisted p; and the associated potential energy U = −mB cos θ runs from the aligned minimum (−mB, stable equilibrium) through zero (perpendicular) to the anti-aligned maximum (+mB, unstable). This torque IS rotating machinery: a compass needle is a dipole seeking alignment with Earth's field; a galvanometer reads current as deflection because τ ∝ I; and an electric MOTOR is a coil-dipole whose alignment torque is never allowed to finish — a commutator reverses the current every half-turn so the 'seek' continues forever. Everything magnetic is dipoles all the way down: bar magnets are domain dipoles summed, and the electron's spin is nature's elementary moment — which is why (echoing the no-monopole law) the dipole, not any magnetic charge, is magnetism's fundamental actor. EARTH itself is the grandest example: the planet behaves as a colossal dipole (moment ~8 × 10²² A·m²) generated not by buried iron bars (the core is above its Curie point!) but by the GEODYNAMO — convecting, rotating conductive liquid iron in the outer core sustaining currents. The dipole is tilted ~11° from the spin axis, so compasses point to magnetic (not geographic) north — the angular difference at your location is the DECLINATION; the field also dips into the ground at an angle (INCLINATION: 0° at the magnetic equator, 90° at the magnetic poles — the reason compass needles are counterweighted by latitude). Two footnotes with consequences: the pole in the Arctic is, magnetically speaking, a SOUTH pole (it must attract compass norths), and the geodynamo's record — striped into oceanic crust — shows the dipole has reversed polarity hundreds of times.
key_ideas: m = NIA (A·m²), direction along the coil axis by the right-hand rule — the dipole moment is a current loop's complete magnetic identity.
Torque τ = mB sin θ twists the dipole toward alignment; energy U = −mB cos θ — aligned is the stable minimum, anti-aligned the unstable maximum: the electric dipole's mathematics transplanted.
The torque is machinery: compass (alignment sought), galvanometer (deflection ∝ I), motor (alignment perpetually re-denied by a commutator flipping I each half-turn).
Dipoles all the way down: domains, atoms, and electron spin are moments — with no monopoles, the dipole is magnetism's elementary actor.
Earth is a tilted dipole (~8 × 10²² A·m², ~11° off the spin axis) powered by the geodynamo — convecting conductive liquid iron, NOT a buried magnet (the core exceeds iron's Curie point).

Navigation fine print: declination (magnetic vs geographic north), inclination (dip angle: 0° at the magnetic equator, 90° at the poles), the Arctic pole is magnetically a SOUTH pole, and the dipole has reversed polarity repeatedly through geological time.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Earth's magnetism comes from a giant permanent magnet of iron in the core.
common_misconceptions.correction: The core is far hotter than iron's Curie temperature (770 °C) — permanent magnetization is impossible there. The field is generated DYNAMICALLY: convection and rotation of conductive liquid iron in the outer core drive electric currents (the geodynamo), a self-sustaining electromagnetic engine, not a buried bar magnet.

common_misconceptions.misconception: The magnetic pole in the Arctic is a magnetic north pole.
common_misconceptions.correction: It is magnetically a SOUTH pole: compass needles' NORTH ends point toward it, and unlike poles attract. 'Magnetic North Pole' is a geographic naming convention. Getting this right is the cleanest test of whether pole attraction is actually understood.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A compass needle points exactly at geographic north.
common_misconceptions.correction: It points along the LOCAL field toward magnetic north, which is offset from geographic north by the declination — several degrees in most places, and mapmakers print it on every serious chart. The needle also tries to DIP into the ground (inclination), which is why needles are counterweighted by hemisphere.

common_misconceptions.misconception: In a uniform field, a current loop experiences a net force pulling it toward the field source.
common_misconceptions.correction: In a UNIFORM field the net FORCE on a dipole is zero — opposite sides of the loop feel equal-and-opposite forces. What it feels is pure TORQUE (τ = mB sin θ). Net forces on dipoles require field GRADIENTS — which is why magnets attract iron strongly only up close, where the field is non-uniform.

visual_teaching_suggestions: The m = NIA builder: a current loop with the right-hand rule curling to the moment arrow, then N stacked turns scaling the arrow — the dipole as a vector you construct.
A torque-and-energy dial: the dipole at angle θ in a uniform field with τ = mB sin θ and U = −mB cos θ plotted together — equilibria (stable/unstable) flagged where torque crosses zero.
The motor half-turn storyboard: coil twisting toward alignment, commutator flipping the current at the crossing, torque renewed — alignment forever denied, rotation forever sustained.
Earth's dipole cutaway: tilted moment arrow, field lines pole to pole, the geodynamo's convecting outer core sketched, the Arctic pole labelled 'magnetically S'.
A declination/inclination pair of maps: compass offset angles across the globe, and dip angle from 0° (magnetic equator) to 90° (poles), with a counterweighted needle inset.

worked_example_blueprint.setup: A rectangular coil of 50 turns, 10 cm × 10 cm, carries 2.0 A in a uniform field B = 0.20 T. (a) Find its magnetic moment. (b) Find the torque when the moment makes 30° with the field, and at what angle torque is maximal. (c) Find the work needed to rotate the coil from alignment (θ = 0°) to anti-alignment (θ = 180°).
